game_name: World Class Track Meet / Stadium Events

design_philosophy.core_loop: Execute rapid footfalls on the control mat to move your virtual athlete through Olympic-inspired track and field events, competing against computer opponents or striving to match Guinness world records
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Physical execution skill (real-world mat stamina and coordination) versus computer opponent performance; false start penalties in tournament mode add resource constraint
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become an Olympic athlete competing in track and field without leaving home; fitness through gaming via the Family Fun Fitness mat accessory
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately positions gaming as exergise—the mat itself is the primary interface, not a controller. The game refuses traditional inputs, making it a statement about active play versus sedentary gaming. Tournament mode enforces strict competitive rules (3 false starts = disqualification), grounding the fantasy in simulated athletic integrity

difficulty.continues_system: Tournament mode: three false starts result in disqualification; no explicit continues documented
difficulty.checkpointing: Tournament mode requires defeating six computer opponents sequentially in both 100M dash and 110M hurdles; no mid-tournament saves documented
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Player controls pace by selecting which row of mat buttons to use (affects runner top speed). Multiple game modes allow casual Olympics competition (no strict penalties) or tournament play (competitive with false start rule). Up to six alternating players can register, suggesting accessibility for varying skill levels

progression.unlock_mechanism: Tournament mode progressively unlocks harder computer opponents; 'The Olympics' mode allows all four events in sequence with cumulative scoring
replayability_hooks: Six different computer opponents to defeat in tournament
Four distinct events with different mechanical demands (dash, hurdles, long jump, triple jump)
Guinness world record reference point as score target in Olympics mode
Mat-based physical skill variability ensures different runs feel different

world_building_techniques: Stadium visual backdrop and Olympic theming
Computer opponent character swaps (noted regional difference: Japanese version had different opponent species)
Real-world record benchmarking (Guinness 1982 world track records) grounds fantasy in athletics authenticity

faction_indicators: Regional/national identity: mat button patterns and opponent nationalities vary by version (Japanese flags replaced with US flags in localization)
danger_signals: False start disqualification mechanic enforces rule adherence—mirrors real athletic consequences
Tournament structure positions each opponent as escalating challenge
environmental_storytelling: Stadium setting evokes Olympic Games grandeur
Hurdle markers, jump distance measurements, and timing displays communicate athletic scoring authenticity
Mat itself becomes the storytelling device: physical exertion = athletic achievement

sources_used: The Cutting Room Floor - Family Fun Fitness: Stadium Events (regional differences, version history, Family Trainer series context)
Wikipedia - Stadium Events (gameplay mechanics, four events, mat controls, tournament mode details, false start rule, Olympics mode with Guinness records, release history, rarity context)
